core.intro: Across Australia the fundamentals of growing jackfruit are the same: a warm, frost-free, sheltered spot, deep soil that drains freely, plenty of water, and room for a big tree. The state sections below cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are. The notes here apply wherever jackfruit will grow.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Australian nurseries sell jackfruit in two broad flesh types. Firm or crisp types, such as Galaxy and Honey Gold, hold their shape and are best eaten fresh out of hand. Soft or melting types, such as Black Gold and the jackfruit and champedak hybrid Cheena, are sweeter and softer, and suit cooking, desserts and freezing. Black Gold and Honey Gold are old north Queensland seedling selections, while Cheena is a compact, easy to open hybrid. Crisp types carry more sticky latex, most of it in the central core. Decide whether you want fresh eating or cooking, then pick a named, grafted variety to match; the in-stock table above shows what is available right now.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree is enough
sections.body: Jackfruit is monoecious, which means each tree carries both male and female flowers, so a single tree will set fruit on its own with no second tree required. The slender male spikes shed pollen that wind and insects move to the female flowers. Growing another tree close by can still lift fruit set and size where pollinating insects are scarce, but it is not necessary for a crop.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Jackfruit wants full sun, warmth and deep, free draining soil, ideally a sandy or alluvial loam around pH 6.0 to 7.5. It will not tolerate wet feet, declining or dying after only a few days in waterlogged ground, yet it also dislikes drought, so the ground must drain well while staying moist. Give the tree a sheltered, frost-free position with room to spread, because it grows large and the limbs are brittle under heavy fruit. Plant young, container grown stock and disturb the roots as little as possible, since jackfruit forms a long taproot and resents transplanting (see our guide on when to plant). Space trees about 6 to 12 metres apart.

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Keep young trees steadily moist and water through dry spells, especially from flowering through fruit development, when a check can drop the crop. An established tree is moderately drought tolerant but crops far better with regular water, and a thick mulch helps hold it, while waterlogging must always be avoided. Feed little and often: in the first year a home garden tree takes about 113 grams (a quarter of a pound) of a complete fertiliser such as a 6:6:6 with minor elements roughly every eight weeks, scaling the amount up as the tree grows. Bearing trees are fed two to three times from flowering through to after harvest, using a balanced to high potassium mix (ratios in the order of 6:6:6 or 8:3:9 are used in Florida), with minor element sprays if the leaves show deficiency. In the Northern Territory the standard advice is simply a mixed fertiliser at the seedling and fruiting stages. Aim to keep the soil around pH 6.0 to 7.5.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Jackfruit is cauliflorous, carrying its huge fruit on the trunk and main branches rather than out among the leaves. A fruit takes roughly 150 to 180 days, about three to eight months depending on variety and climate, to mature after flowering. It is ready when the skin lightens from green to a yellowish brown, the blunt spines flatten, spread further apart and give to gentle pressure, a strong sweet aroma develops, and a tap produces a dull hollow sound rather than a solid one. A grafted, named tree usually starts bearing in about three to four years, while a seedling takes four to eight years, and longer again in cooler districts.

sections.heading: Latex, ripe and green: how to use it
sections.body: Jackfruit is the largest tree fruit in the world, commonly 10 to 25 kilograms and occasionally over 50. Ripe, the golden bulbs (arils) around the seeds are eaten fresh and taste richly sweet. Picked green and unripe, the whole fruit is cooked as a vegetable, the use behind its reputation as a plant based meat substitute. The large seeds are good boiled or roasted, with a chestnut or nutty flavour. Opening a fruit releases copious sticky white latex, so rub cooking oil over your hands and the knife first; most of the latex sits in the central core, so cut around it. Ripe fruit does not keep long, so use or chill it promptly.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling. Jackfruit does not come true from seed, so seedlings give unpredictable fruit and bear years later, and the seed is short lived and cannot be stored or posted dry. A grafted tree fruits sooner, around three to four years, and to a known type. Check the label for the cultivar name and whether it is a firm or a soft type, and choose to suit how you plan to eat it.

faqs.q: How big does a jackfruit tree get, and can I keep it smaller?
faqs.a: Left alone a jackfruit reaches about 9 to 12 metres, taller still in the deep tropics, but it takes pruning well. Cut it back after each harvest to hold it to a pickable 3 to 5 metres, which also makes netting and spraying easier. A young tree can also be kept in a large tub for a few years before it wants the ground.

faqs.q: What does ripe jackfruit taste like?
faqs.a: The ripe bulbs are sweet and fruity, often described as a mix of banana, mango and pineapple with a faint bubblegum note. Firm types eat crisp and chewy, soft types melt in the mouth. Green, unripe jackfruit is bland and meaty in texture, which is why it is used as a plant based meat substitute.

faqs.q: Is jackfruit related to breadfruit, figs and champedak?
faqs.a: Yes. Jackfruit is an Artocarpus in the mulberry and fig family (Moraceae), so its close relatives include breadfruit and champedak, and more distantly figs and mulberries. Like a fig, what looks like one fruit is really many small fruits fused into a single aggregate, which is why the flesh is made of separate bulbs.

faqs.q: Why is my tree's fruit lumpy or only half filled?
faqs.a: Patchy, lumpy fruit usually means the flowers were only partly pollinated, often after still, wet or very hot weather at flowering, or when few bees and other insects were about. It tends to even out as the tree matures, and a second tree close by, or gently brushing pollen between flowers by hand, helps.

WA.intro: Growing jackfruit in Western Australia. Jackfruit is a tropical tree, so in WA it belongs in the warm, frost-free north; around Perth it is a tub or glasshouse curiosity rather than a dependable orchard tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jackfruit needs warmth, freedom from frost and steady moisture, which WA only offers in its tropical and subtropical north. The populous south-west has a Mediterranean climate of cool wet winters and hot dry summers, close to the opposite of what the tree wants, so in and around Perth it is grown as a potted or protected specimen and crops only lightly.

sections.heading: Where jackfruit grows in WA
sections.body: The natural home is the far north: the Ord River Irrigation Area around Kununurra and the wider Kimberley, where the state's tropical research station grows jackfruit and mango, plus the Broome coast. The Gascoyne around Carnarvon is a warm plantation district that grows mango and banana, but its cooler winters make jackfruit more marginal there than in the Kimberley.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: In the tropical north the fruit ripens across a long warm-season window. Pick each fruit when its skin turns from pale green to a brownish yellow and it sounds hollow when tapped, then handle it gently because it is heavy and bruises.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: The dry Kimberley air keeps fungal problems lower than in the humid east, but blossom and fruit rot can still strike young fruit during wet-season rain, so prune for airflow. Watch also for borers, mealybugs and scale, and for root rot wherever the ground stays wet.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine requires live plants from interstate to be permitted on the WA Organism List and usually inspected, and often treated and certified, before entry. Many eastern nurseries will not post live trees here as a result, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: Can I grow a jackfruit in Perth?
faqs.a: You can keep one as a tub or glasshouse plant in the warmest, most sheltered, sun-trapped corner you have, moved under cover for the coolest months. Expect a smaller tree and only the occasional light crop rather than the big fruit of the tropics. On Perth's sandy soils, dig in compost, mulch well and use a soil wetting agent.

faqs.q: Which jackfruit should I choose for the WA tropics?
faqs.a: In the Kimberley and the Gascoyne a named, grafted tree is the safe choice, and a firm or crisp type that is eaten fresh suits the heat and travels well. Keep the water up through fruit fill in the dry. For a Perth pot, pick a tree you can prune hard to keep compact.

QLD.intro: Growing jackfruit in Queensland. Queensland is the heart of the Australian jackfruit scene, from the wet tropics of the far north down through the warm subtropical coast.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The humid, frost-free tropical north suits jackfruit perfectly, with warm nights, high rainfall and no need for any winter chill. Warm coastal and subtropical south-east Queensland can grow it too in frost-free spots, but cooler inland and elevated districts, and any frost, hold it back. Jackfruit is one of the emerging tropical fruits being developed in northern Australia.

sections.heading: Where jackfruit grows in QLD
sections.body: The strongholds are the wet tropics and Cassowary Coast around Innisfail, Tully and Babinda, the Atherton Tablelands, and the Mareeba and Mutchilba district inland. Government trial and demonstration plantings have run at Bellenden Ker, Atherton, Mutchilba and Townsville, and the crop reaches down the Central Queensland coast around Yeppoon.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: In the tropics the fruit ripens across a long warm season, with the main flush in the warmer months, each fruit maturing three to eight months after it flowers. Judge ripeness by the hollow sound, the softening, spreading spines and the strong aroma.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Wet-season humidity is the main driver. Rhizopus blossom and fruit rot spoils flowers and young fruit in warm, rainy weather, so prune for airflow so the fruit dries after rain. Watch too for shoot and bark borers, mealybugs and scale, pink disease and root rots in wet ground. Despite its name, Queensland fruit fly is not a recorded pest of jackfruit, whose thick rind resists it.

faqs.q: Can you grow jackfruit in Brisbane or on the Sunshine Coast?
faqs.a: Yes, in a warm, frost-free, sheltered spot near the coast. Start with a named grafted tree, give it the sunniest position you have, and protect it from any cold snap while young. Crops are dependable on the frost-free coast but chancier in cold inland or elevated gardens.

faqs.q: Is jackfruit a good backyard tree in the far north?
faqs.a: It is one of the most rewarding: fast, evergreen, productive, and useful as a shade or windbreak tree that also feeds you. The one caution is the weight of the fruit, so site the tree where a falling jackfruit of many kilograms cannot land on a car, a path or a roof.

NSW.intro: Growing jackfruit in New South Wales. Jackfruit suits only the warm, frost-free far north coast of NSW; frost is what stops it further south and inland.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The subtropical far north coast is just warm and frost-free enough near the sea to ripen jackfruit, but the tree is set back or killed by even a light frost. That rules out the tablelands, the inland and the cooler central and southern coast, and it means young trees in particular must be kept frost-free.

sections.heading: Where jackfruit grows in NSW
sections.body: Plantings cluster in the Northern Rivers: the Tweed Valley, Murwillumbah, Kyogle and Lismore, in warm pockets close to the coast. The locally raised cultivar Kyogle Gold comes from this district. Jackfruit becomes increasingly marginal as you move south.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: This far south the season runs later and shorter than in the tropics, with most fruit ripening through late summer and autumn, around February to May. Each fruit still needs three to eight months from flowering to reach maturity.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: The humid north-coast summer brings the same blossom and fruit rot as Queensland, worst in warm, wet spells, along with borers, mealybugs and scale. Good airflow through the canopy and clearing fallen fruit keep the worst of it down.

faqs.q: How far south can you grow jackfruit in NSW?
faqs.a: Only where winters are reliably frost-free, which in practice is the warm coastal fringe of the state's far north. Move away from the immediate coast, or onto higher or more southerly ground, and an occasional frost will kill a young tree, so a snug, sea-influenced microclimate is essential.

faqs.q: Which jackfruit suits a Northern Rivers backyard?
faqs.a: A named, grafted tree planted in your warmest, most sheltered corner. Locally selected types such as Kyogle Gold are at home in the subtropics, and whether you choose a firm or a soft type is down to taste. Mulch well and keep young trees protected through their first winters.

VIC.intro: Growing jackfruit in Victoria. Victoria's cool temperate climate and regular winter frosts mean jackfruit cannot be grown outdoors here as a fruit tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jackfruit is frost-tender: its leaves are damaged at around zero degrees and the tree itself is killed a couple of degrees below. Victoria has cold, frosty winters across the state, and Melbourne's coldest months average about six degrees overnight, well under what the tree can take, so an outdoor jackfruit will not survive a winter, let alone fruit.

sections.heading: Even the warmest districts frost
sections.body: The warm, dry north-west around Mildura is Victoria's best fruit country, yet its winter nights still average only about four degrees with frosts, so there is no frost-free pocket anywhere in the state where a tropical tree like jackfruit could live outdoors.

sections.heading: Under glass only
sections.body: The single way to keep a jackfruit alive in Victoria is a heated glasshouse, a conservatory or a warm, bright indoor spot, held above frost all winter. Even then the tree grows large and needs sustained tropical heat and humidity to set fruit, so under cover it stays a leafy specimen rather than a cropping tree.

sections.heading: Where Australian jackfruit is grown
sections.body: There are no commercial jackfruit groves in Victoria. The fruit you see for sale is grown in the tropical north, in Queensland and around Darwin in the Northern Territory, then sent south, so most Victorian gardeners buy the tree as a novelty rather than for the kitchen.

faqs.q: Can I grow jackfruit in a pot in Melbourne?
faqs.a: You can keep a young tree in a tub if you move it somewhere warm and bright for winter and never let it chill, but it will stay small and is very unlikely ever to fruit this far south. Treat it as a foliage novelty, not a source of fruit.

faqs.q: What can I grow in Victoria instead of jackfruit?
faqs.a: For a frost-prone garden, hardier subtropicals like feijoa or loquat, or a cool-tolerant citrus, give reliable fruit where jackfruit simply cannot. Save jackfruit for a trip north, or for a heated glasshouse if you are set on trying.
